text: What have we got here other than retreat? This was for Lara. So I'll skip it in in case Lara comes. But then I'll come back to it instead. I'll talk to Brad and Tara. So Brad and Tara have finished their shed and what do you know, they've filled it. What a surprise. So then now they're asking the question whether they should be realistically thinking about a warehouse maybe. I've got some questions for you guys.
I would like to understand what you feel you would require the warehouse space for.
I guess my, my main concern is this tender we put in is where to actually build the thing because I haven't actually got the space to build it. Yep.
We want to make big concerns. It would definitely make our workflow easier having some dedicated machinery for those sort of processes instead of I can't really borrow a friend's table saw. Yep.
Sort of having my own things like that. That would be the main concern, main benefits. And then it also would separate from sort of like. It gets all the employees out of our personal kitchen.
Yes. Does it gets them to serve their own separation thing?

I think there are pros and cons. So some of the pros would be those things that you've clearly identified. Some of the cons would be these are generally commercial leases and therefore have quite a long period of time on the lease.

And when factoring the costs into the overheads of the business, does that weigh up as an opportunity that we need to be looking at just yet or is it an opportunity we should be considering a little further down the track once we've got a more regular turnover of this style of work?

I think that, I think you're absolutely right for the kind of work that you do. I think that would be very advantageous to have the space to be able to do a lot of the pre assemble and to be able to do a lot of the work off so site or away from the home and then transport it and fit it where necessary.

Unfortunately it comes down to the costs and whether that's a viable option for you for a long period of time because generally it's a, either a three or a five year lease with the option for another couple years which can be quite expensive and quite challenging then to build into your pricing.
Now if we had a really consistent flow of the A class clients that you guys are working towards, it'd be a no brainer. Yes.

At the moment it might be just looking at whether the capacity is there on a financial basis to be able to do that ongoing for three years now, with the shed that you do have at home, is it full of your personal stuff or full of your work stuff?

It's a little bit of mixed match at the moment, but it's not even finished yet. Still working through it, but yes, it's sort of. Yeah, it's definitely better than what we were in, but it's definitely small.
Still not right. Yeah.
So I wonder if there are some other possibilities that could be considered. Brad. So like Scott says, build another shed, build a bigger shed.

Or do you have the opportunity to hire a storage facility that you can move some of your personal stuff in so that you've got the space to do that work in the shed that you have at the moment? They're generally month to month leases. Like a storage shed is a small period of time and a very small investment. I've still.
I've got a massive shed, doesn't fit all of my stuff. So I've still got a storage shed and I think I pay maybe 390 bucks a month for a single car garage kind of size.

It's off site. We rarely ever visit it. It's quite frankly just storage for shit I probably need to sell. However, it means I've got space in the shed that I have here to be able to do what I want to do here.

Perhaps that's another option that you could look into because they're generally much less expensive and you don't have a fixed term of a lease. We've got to fit the car here, Scott. Yeah, there's. I mean that's what we've done in past is we've. I mean, we ended up in a film studio. Cool.
Last time we just rented one for the week and sort of went from there. But I mean they get up there as well. They're like two grand a week. Yeah. So it eats into stuff pretty quickly. Yeah, it does.
And this is a. Looking like a. Like a two and a half month.

Build. So yeah, I would be looking at what you can do with a storage shed because you can work from a storage. Well depending on the agreement. You can often work from a storage shed as well. It doesn't need to just be storage space.
There's a guy in our storage shed that's building an airplane and so he goes there every day to build the airplane. So I feel as though there'd be the possibility to do that.

I think we're probably looking for a temporary solution for now and then a longer term solution once we've got that better quality client that isn't belly aching when we are asking for a contract to be signed.

Or isn't bellyaching about chipped doors that are not significant because they've created the problem. Yep, cool beans. I think long term this should turn up in your goals for next year. It's just getting the timing right financially for you. Yep.

Yeah. So. Well this is Victoria. I'm not too sure what it's like in other states but the lease agreements are normally basically a five year lease. Generally speaking, five year lease with get yourself a couple of get out of jail free options. So the first time I did it was a five year lease.

They have to guarantee me the five years but I can opt out after the first year and after the third year but they can't kick me out unless they want to sell it I guess. But so now I just do fixed five year and there's no opt out option because I know I want to be there.

But the first time to have that 12 month opt out. Yeah, but they can, they'll you, you get to make that call, not them. Yeah, it's really good because it costs a lot to, especially in three phase power and that cost a lot to wire into. You get a shell basically and you've got to do all that yourself. Not really nothing with kind of mod.

Maybe she can jump on again when she comes back. She was asking about grants with machinery.
Oh yes, they are a possibility. Dan has had this opportunity.

The only one that I'm aware of now is if you can call it like an art. If you're R and D developing something, there's a grant grant for that at the moment, machinery and stuff. So if you're developing a new product or some sort of, you know, something that you can patent, there's about a 50 grand grant for that.

It's a. Yeah, the one I did was a bit different specifically for hardwood, but that was 120 grand grant and that took about two years and a lot of barking around. But I got there in the end so definitely worth looking.
And I think there's actually businesses that specifically help you go for grants and know what grants are out there.

There are, yeah. Thank you, Dan. Sometimes not to be overlooked are the little local government grants that you can come across. They do a lot of small business grants because they want to keep business local rather than it being exported. And by exported I mean just outside of their local government area.
So I think it would be really helpful to well first do a basic Google search and see what you can come up with or ask ChatGPT to do a search for you. Second part of that is I agree with Dan.
If you can't find anything that's easy because there's a specific way you need to write a little bit like a tender for you to qualify for the grants as well.

You may actually consider having one of those grant writing companies put something together for you if there is something available and they can do a lot of the research to figure out how or what you could qualify for as well.

Because it might be that, you know, you can get A grant for something that's totally unrelated and then divert other funds from the business into paying for that piece of machinery whilst the grant pays for your marketing or pays for your coaching or pays for your your accountant fees or, you know, there's lots of different ways that you could skin that cat but take somebody with the know how to be able to find it for you and then pull it together.
It's a good question though. Yeah, sounds good.

I think so Jackie, after we spoke about it this morning, I think it'll be hopefully a little bit easier for you to tie it together. Remembering that a training plan doesn't need to be elaborate for anybody that's considering creating them.

And I advocate for you all to be creating them in January with your team. Training plans can simply be dot points of what you agree to be teaching your team member over the next 12 months. Everybody gets a document you both sign.

It feels official to them, makes them feel a little bit more secure and like they are valued and they've got a great place and we're investing in their training and then we sign off as they reach each of those competencies or they have that training and they get the skills that we require them to develop.
So don't make it hard on yourselves. Make it as easy as possible. Good one, Jackie. All right, next question for today.

So what's the main objection that you're being handed?

Finding that our quote, our 30 day quote process lapses without any. Frustratingly sometimes without any feedback from the client because they're waiting on XYZ to finally get the project to go ahead.
Is there a way that you can minimise that frustration? For example, having some base level pricing that they can access without you having.

To do a full quote for our A classes? Yes, they already do have that as well. Which typically we can. We then only need to quote for those clients if the, if the job scope is out of the, the norm. Yep.
For them. So yeah, they, they do. They're, they're fine. It's just the, the extras.
So we're talking more the householder more than a tradesperson. Okay. Yep. Yeah.
Is there a way we can do some education prior to doing the quote so they come back to you last?

Maybe I think like one, one particular quote or client that I can. That's in front of mind. Kept saying, kept trying to kick the can down the road with us to say look, we're waiting on other quotes of the industry to know what our total build price is at.
And our response was well, just sign the quote with us so that we can convert your job from a quote to a project in our system. Doesn't mean that you're paying us any money.

It's just that you've locked in our price because otherwise we'll have to re quote if this happens at further down the line and then if you go ahead with the process then great, we can start the assessments, we can start our job.

Or if it doesn't go, if it doesn't happen, if you get astronomical builder quotes and you want to put shelve the project for a little while, then that's fine, we just close it. Close the project down in our system.

But they didn't want to do that. They didn't want to commit to signing our T's and C's until they got all pieces of the puzzle. And then we re quoted that job three times because it kept going over that 30 day process. And then I just said to him sign this last and final quote or we just can't help.

I think we've got to do two things, Scott. I think there's an opportunity for some education around the timing of your quote. Like come to us when you have the other quotes in place and you know you're moving here with your project. Project. I think we could do some sales training for your team to try and get more agreement, more buy in early on.

And that's just changing the way we ask the questions that we ask. And then I think finally we only ever do one quote until there's an acceptance and then we can re quote at the significant time. Yeah. Okay.
Just to minimise the time because that would be quite time consuming for your team.
Yeah, yeah. Keep asking me questions. Cool.

A lot of that you can do with the way you deal with them right from day one, of course, your processes, and making sure that they're always consistently followed. That is a concern. However, that doesn't mean that you can't manage the client.

Like interior design.
Is this person providing plans for you?

Not as in the interior designer. Oh, they're not plans, mate. That's like done up on sketch or something. It's dead. Yeah. So it's interesting. Like tries to throw a skylight in the middle of an insulated panel roof and you're like, yeah, work mate.
Definitely. Just be careful.

Interior designers come with their own set of life rules. They're very different people. And, and I'm speaking, you know, with a fair bit of experience in this area and very kindly. We don't understand building. We only understand shapes. Shapes and colors and patterns and fabrics and things. We do not understand the science of building. The only time I've seen successful outcomes with interior designers is when they work closely with the builder.

And so if she's asking you questions, it's because they literally don't know. Brenda. Building designers were probably my favorite to work with, but they're a little bit like higher end architects. They're selling a dream that you then, as the builder need to go and deliver. The hard news for this is going to cost 10 times what your budget.
Was because they've got no responsibility form. Yeah.

And that's one of the things. Like we know that she only had a budget of 750, 800, and we've been telling her your drawings are going to go a million. So now we've sort of broken it down into stage one and stage two. So one stage one hits the threshold of budget and stage two doesn't happen.

Yep. Yeah, yeah, it's. It is an added layer of complexity. You're going to need a lot of support from all of us to get through this one without losing all your hair, Brendan. And it's an hour and a half away.
Oh, my gosh. On a citrus farm. So it'll be pretty lonely.
Oh, you will be lonely. She won't be though. She'll be there every minute of the day checking on what you're doing, more than likely. Yeah.
Oh, that's okay. We're all here for you. And it sounds like a fantastic opportunity. Sometimes we've got to push through the pain.

And part of the price of working with high level clients often is their degree of involvement, how much they want to control and as your drafty has pointed out, needs a firm hand right from the very beginning, setting the expectations, ensuring that you keep doing so moving forward.

Don't let anybody live through a reno of that size in the premise they need to leave. Having parameters about when they come on site, for example, is super important. Otherwise they go on the weekend and they think everything's crap.
You will have seen those builder groups on Facebook where everyone's whinging about what the builder has and hasn't done and there's no sheets on the wall yet. Like it's. It's quite ridiculous.

So I think just having really tight boundaries in place and even preparing a document now about how they engage with you moving forward can be super beneficial. You got the time to do it while she's playing with the interior designer.

So putting that in place would, you know, maybe there's an agreement that you'll meet her on site once a fortnight for a one hour meeting. Like put time limits around it so it's really clear with her expectations moving forward.

I got paid, it was fine. The things they want to pay us for sometimes are Amazing. You want to pay us to do this? Okay, cool. Sure. No worries. Whatever it takes. Yeah. Yeah.

Thank you, Brad. Thanks, Dan. Good to see you. Yes. Miles has worked with his share of interior designers and I reckon he would have a story or two to share. Jackie. Yes. Well, that's the end of my question.

All right, I'll go back and answer Lara's so that she can find it on the recording. And if anybody's got any other questions, you can drop them into the chat. Otherwise, we'll finish up after this one. Lara was asking how we handle staff meetings when the crew are all out on different sites.

This can be really challenging and we can do it a couple of different ways. We have this amazing invention that you're all on today called zoom. Zoom. So we can most definitely host a zoom meeting for a toolbox meeting.

We can also do it over the phone if we really have to. Like if they. I know their locations are probably not that rural, but if they're more rural, you can just get everybody to phone in. The most important part of doing anything online or over the phone is checking for comprehension.

The difference between seeing people in front of you is you get to observe their body language. So you know when they've zoned out, you know exactly the point at which you've lost them in the conversation and they're not listening anymore. You can't do that over the phone. And it's hard over zoom.

I mean, even in a room this big, I can see most of you, but it's very hard for me to keep up with all of you and make sure that I'm keeping you entertained or engaged in a one on one. It's different. It's not so bad because usually it's only one or two of you and I'm only closely watching you there.

And you tend to keep your cameras quite broad for me, so I can see your body language. But for your team, it's not going to be that way. It's likely to be on a mobile. So I advocate that you find a way to check for comprehension of what was discussed.
So that might be that you ask them all to vox you through an update on what was discussed and how they're going to action that in their work life.

Or it might be. I mean, you could do something fun like a quiz. Ask them a question on the spot, ask everybody a different question. Just check to make sure they've actually heard and comprehended what you spoke about. And then making sure, as always, whether it's in person or online. Or on the phone, you get a sign off. Sign off is important if ever we have a safety issue and you can demonstrate that it's been spoken about many times in your toolbox talks. They're also.

Toolbox talks are a requirement by law. I think you meant to have had them once a month or maybe it's once a week, but they are a requirement. So having that sign off can demonstrate that you are doing it consistently. But I don't mind how you do it so long as they're being done and you're checking comprehension.

Anybody else got any amazing other ideas that perhaps we haven't thought about? Maybe you could do like a. What's the iPhone call thing that you'll do where you do it over WhatsApp, where you can have a video call and you can patch other people in. We often have family WhatsApp calls.
Everybody gets on from all over the world.

I just tell my staff when I'm onboarding them that there's just no excuse for not communications these days. We've got Team Voxer, Facebook messenger, text message, phone calls, emails, Carrie Pigeon. Like, we just got, like, there's. There's no way that they can't contact me or another staff. Oh, and then obviously teams with the two. Two offline people. Yeah. So then we.

We just provide that. We do a. We do a Monday morning technical team meeting and two of those people are on, on the TV for those. So they all just locked in the calendar each Monday morning and they will just chime in.

We'll have a laugh. So spend a bit of time together. Might even go into some breakout rooms. So that's on the 12th and then our last big event for this year is Wednesday 17th December when we have our review preview session. So it's your opportunity to look backwards. How did the year go?
Because it's not something that we do. We don't often pat ourselves on the back.

So we're going to get really clear on what changed for us this year and then we're going to start to get excited about what's coming coming next year because there's quite a lot of new stuff coming next year for us in terms of sessions and the layout for the year.

So we're going to give you the insight to all of those, lock them into your calendar and start to get excited. That'll be the last big session we do for December. We're all offline. We set you free for a couple of weeks.
